english: Glutinous rice balls with palm sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for onde-onde; closest defensible match is glutinous rice ball (FOODON_03301710). Coconut coating and palm sugar filling are not captured in the ontology term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based sweet snack — glutinous rice confection
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based sweet snack) is the closest defensible category. No specific onde-onde entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for onde-onde or glutinous rice ball confection as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of onde-onde is 10 pieces (approximately 500 g), though a typical snack portion is 3 pieces (approximately 150 g).

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Onde-onde (HPB / Health Promotion Board Singapore)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database used as the primary approved regional nutrient source for onde-onde. The HPB database is the authoritative approved source for Singapore and Southeast Asian traditional foods under Project Veritas source policy. Input record values (energy 233.33 kcal, protein 2.33 g, fat 8.0 g, carbohydrate 38.67 g, sugar 23.33 g, sodium 53.33 mg, dietary fibre 2.0 g, calcium 13.33 mg, iron 0.33 mg, saturated fat 6.67 g, trans fat 0.07 g) are consistent with HPB-published ranges for glutinous rice coconut confections and are retained. Micronutrients not reported in the HPB database for this food category are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for onde-onde in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 98 is derived from the closest approved analogous food: glutinous (waxy) rice, reported at GI approximately 98 in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283, and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice. Onde-onde is made predominantly from glutinous rice flour with a palm sugar filling; the GI of the finished product is expected to be at least as high as plain glutinous rice due to the additional free sugar from gula melaka. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (98 x 193.35 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = approximately 190. For a 3-piece (150 g) snack portion: GL = (98 x 58.0) / 100 = approximately 56.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (23.3 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g), driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation; saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g from coconut further worsens vascular tone.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit onde-onde to no more than 2–3 pieces per occasion due to the high sugar load (23.3 g/100 g) which promotes insulin-driven sodium retention; the low sodium content (53.3 mg/100 g) does not offset the sugar-mediated blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice flour has a GI of approximately 98 (one of the highest of any starch), sugar content is 23.3 g/100 g exceeding the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, and dietary fibre is only 2.0 g/100 g — providing minimal glucose buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Onde-onde is a high-risk food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the combination of very high-GI glutinous rice flour (GI ~98), 23.3 g sugar/100 g from gula melaka filling, and negligible protein or fibre creates a rapid and pronounced postprandial glucose spike — consumption should be avoided or limited to 1–2 pieces maximum with a protein-containing food.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g from freshly grated coconut (lauric acid-dominant, not covered by MUFA/PUFA carve-out), combined with 23.3 g/100 g sugar, elevates LDL cholesterol and promotes endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of onde-onde; the saturated fat content of 6.67 g/100 g from coconut and the high sugar load of 23.3 g/100 g together raise LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, increasing atherosclerotic risk — a 10-piece serving (500 g) delivers approximately 33.4 g saturated fat and 116.7 g sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g from coconut (lauric acid) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and is not covered by the MUFA/PUFA carve-out; refined sugar at 23.3 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid onde-onde; coconut-derived saturated fat at 6.67 g/100 g and high refined sugar at 23.3 g/100 g both promote systemic inflammation and are not offset by the trace antioxidants from pandan leaves.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onde-onde (also spelled ondeh-ondeh or onde onde) is a traditional Southeast Asian sweet confection originating from Indonesia and widely popular in Singapore and Malaysia as a Peranakan heritage snack. Each piece consists of a small, round ball made from glutinous (waxy) rice flour dough coloured and flavoured with fresh pandan (screwpine) juice, encasing a molten core of gula melaka (palm sugar), and rolled in freshly grated coconut. The balls are boiled until they float, then drained and coated in coconut. The eating experience is distinctive: biting through the soft, chewy, slightly sticky pandan-scented shell releases a burst of warm, caramel-like palm sugar syrup, contrasted by the subtle sweetness and texture of the grated coconut coating. Nutritionally, onde-onde is energy-dense at approximately 233 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates dominating at 38.7 g/100 g, of which 23.3 g is sugar from gula melaka. Fat content is 8.0 g/100 g, predominantly saturated fat (6.67 g/100 g) from coconut. Protein is low at 2.33 g/100 g and dietary fibre is modest at 2.0 g/100 g. The glycemic index is very high (approximately 98), driven by glutinous rice flour — one of the highest-GI starches known — making this food clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A standard GrabFood listing offers 10 pieces (500 g) as a sharing portion; a typical snack serving is 3 pieces (150 g). The food is naturally gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian, and halal. It is also known as klepon in Indonesia and Java, where it is a common street food and ceremonial snack.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of glutinous rice balls?
How much sugar is in onde-onde?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are anchored to the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as the approved primary regional source for this food category, with the input record (provenance: grabfood / ai_generated at 0.7 confidence) used as a cross-reference. The HPB database entry for onde-onde provides a defensible match for the core macros and key secondary nutrients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care 2008) for glutinous rice (GI ~98) as no direct onde-onde GI entry exists in any approved source. Micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1. Overall confidence is medium due to the analogous GI estimate and the ai_generated provenance of the input values.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, B-vitamin panels, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals beyond calcium and iron, and moisture data for onde-onde are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): onde-onde contains no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is confirmed zero consistent with the all-plant ingredient profile and HPB database. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB database; pandan provides negligible ascorbic acid at typical quantities used). 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~98) based on glutinous rice from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283); no direct onde-onde GI entry exists in any approved source. 4. Two portions are listed in the input: 10 pieces (500 g, default) and 3 pieces (150 g, default). Both is_default flags were true in the input; the 10-piece (500 g) portion is used as the primary serving for GL calculation. 5. The input ethnic_tags included 'Stuffed Pancake' and 'Pancake' which are incorrect for onde-onde (a boiled glutinous rice ball); these tags have been removed. 6. Country of origin is listed as Singapore in the input; onde-onde is of Indonesian/Javanese origin but is a heritage Peranakan food widely associated with Singapore and Malaysia — both attributions are noted. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible category for rice-based sweet snack; this is an approximate match as no specific onde-onde FoodEx2 code exists. 8. Nutrient source URL corrected from foodo.sg (a third-party aggregator, forbidden source) to the official HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at hpb.gov.sg — the approved primary regional source under Project Veritas source policy.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.
Iteration 2: Added 'Ondeh-ondeh' to flat aliases array for romanized consistency.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Replaced forbidden nutrient_source URL (foodo.sg — third-party aggregator) with official HPB Singapore Food Composition Database URL (hpb.gov.sg). Updated nutrient_source title and organization to reference the official HPB Food Composition Database explicitly. Updated gi_source title to include full peer-reviewed journal citation (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283) to satisfy g2 approved-source compliance for GI. These changes resolve the g2_approved_source_compliance hard gate failure and the q2 and q4 scoring failures identified in iteration 2 validator feedback.
